reasoning: The code path is technically reachable: an authenticated administrator POSTs to the AJAX handler, passes check_ajax_referer('wpcaptcha_run_tool') and current_user_can('manage_options'), then supplies captcha_secret_key and captcha_response values that are sanitized with sanitize_text_field() (which strips HTML tags and invalid UTF-8 but does NOT encode '&', '=', or '#'). Those values are concatenated directly into the Google siteverify URL at lines 173 and 186 without URL-encoding, so an admin-controlled value like 'key&extra=val' would inject an extra query parameter into the outbound request. However, the finding does not constitute a real vulnerability: (1) the actor is a fully authenticated WordPress administrator with manage_options capability, who already has unrestricted control over the site including the reCAPTCHA configuration — they can modify the keys directly via settings without any URL injection; (2) the injection target is a hardcoded Google API endpoint, so there is no SSRF to an attacker-controlled host and no sensitive data exfiltration path; (3) the only effect is that the admin could malform their own reCAPTCHA verification call, a self-harm scenario with no privilege escalation or cross-user impact; (4) WordPress security convention treats administrator-only findings as out-of-scope. The auth guard at line 22 (current_user_can + wp_send_json_error which calls wp_die) and nonce check at line 20 are both effective. is_real=false because the finding has no exploitable security consequence beyond what the actor already controls.
